title: Śledzie w oleju
subtitle: Polish pickled herring in oil with onion
excerpt: Salted herring fillets rinsed, cut into pieces, and steeped in sunflower oil with thin-sliced raw onion, black peppercorns, allspice, and bay leaves. A classic Polish appetiser — sharp, savoury, and finished after two days in the refrigerator.
sourceNotes: Śledzie w oleju (herring in oil) is one of the most common herring preparations in Polish cookery. Salted or pickled herring has been a staple in Poland since the medieval period, when it was traded extensively through the Baltic. The oil-and-onion preparation is specifically Polish; Swedish and Dutch counterparts typically use cream or mustard. This version uses readily available fresh herring or packaged salted herring.

recipe.batchNotes: Keeps refrigerated for up to 5 days. Flavour improves after the second day.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Must be made at least 24 hours ahead; 48 hours is better.

content.text: Polish herring preparations appear on every Christmas Eve table and at most celebratory meals. This version steeps the fish in oil with raw onion and spices for at least two days, which mellows the sharpness of the brine while the onion softens in the oil and absorbs the herring's flavour. The result is mild enough for those who normally avoid salted fish.

You can use whole salted herring (maatjes or rollmops), or ready-to-use pickled herring fillets from the chilled section. If using very salty whole herring, soak in cold water for 12 hours, changing the water twice, before proceeding. Mild ready-to-use fillets need only a brief rinse.

items.prepNote: pickled or salted fillets, cut into 3 cm pieces

content.text: Rinse the {{herring}} fillets and pat dry. Cut each fillet into pieces about 3 cm long and set aside.

In a clean jar or lidded container, layer the herring pieces with the {{onion}} slices, {{peppercorns-black}}, {{allspice}}, and {{bay-leaves}}. Whisk together the {{sunflower-oil}}, {{cider-vinegar}}, and sugar if using, then pour over the fish and onion. The oil should cover everything; add a little more oil if needed. Close the lid, refrigerate, and leave for at least 48 hours before serving.

items.symptom: Herring still very salty after steeping
items.cause: Heavily salted whole herring not soaked for long enough before use
items.fix: If using heavily salted herring, soak in cold water for 12 to 24 hours before use, changing the water at least twice. Mild packaged fillets do not need soaking

items.cause: Onion sliced too thick or fish-to-oil ratio too low
items.fix: Slice the onion paper-thin; the oil softens thin slices more effectively. Ensure the onion is submerged in the oil

items.cause: Refrigerator temperature causes oil to cloud — normal
items.fix: Allow to stand at room temperature for 15 minutes before serving; the oil will clarify

content.text: Herring in one form or another is obligatory on the Polish Christmas Eve table (Wigilia), alongside twelve other meatless dishes that represent the twelve apostles. Śledzie w oleju is the simplest and most common. The other herring preparations at the Polish table include herring in soured cream (śledź w śmietanie) and herring with apple and mustard. All are made ahead and served cold, which makes them practical for a day of many courses. They keep well and arrive at the table without any last-minute cooking.
